title: onCancel sets cancelled=true then calls finishSession which checks ended, creating a window where a concurrent run() finally path can also call finishSession before cancelled is visible
description: onCancel() sets this.cancelled = true and this.paused = false, posts the metric and protocol message, then calls finishSession('cancelled'). Meanwhile the run() loop's finally block always calls finishSession(this.state). finishSession is guarded by this.ended, so only the first caller wins — but onCancel() posts the decode_cancelled message BEFORE finishSession sets this.ended = true (line 219 calls finishSession after line 217 posts the message). If run()'s finally races in between (the worker event loop is single-threaded but the sequence spans two microtask turns: onCancel is async and awaits disposeActiveDecoder after finishSession), the cancel message is correct, but if onCancel is called after run's finally has already set ended=true via a different state, the guard on line 208 returns early without posting decode_cancelled — silently dropping it. This is a TOCTOU: the check `if (this.ended || this.cancelled)` at line 208 reads ended, but ended is set inside finishSession which is called later by the same path.

category: unawaited promise / fire-and-forget on error path
title: disposeActiveDecoder() is called with void in failSession/postBudgetExceeded without awaiting, so a subsequent onCancel that also calls disposeActiveDecoder may enter a second dispose race
description: failSession() and postBudgetExceeded() both call `void this.disposeActiveDecoder()`. disposeActiveDecoder() is idempotent via disposePromise, but the void means the caller does not wait for the dispose to complete. If onCancel() arrives immediately after failSession() returns (before the microtask chain finishes), it will see ended=true (so it returns early) — this is fine. However, in run()'s finally block, disposeActiveDecoder is awaited AFTER finishSession, meaning the finally's await could race with the void-dispatched dispose started from failSession or postBudgetExceeded. The idempotent guard (disposePromise !== null) protects the actual dispose call, but the Promise returned by the second caller is the same shared promise. This is safe by design but the pattern is fragile: if any caller needs to await disposal completion, they must account for the shared-promise pattern. The real risk is that if decoder.dispose() rejects, the rejection surfaces only to the first awaiter; the void call in failSession eats the rejection completely.

title: feedEncoder's finish path checks isTerminal() then awaits encoder.finish() without re-checking finished flag after the wait, allowing a concurrent onFinish() to be ignored if it arrives while onFinish was already processed
description: In feedEncoder(), after `await encoder.pushPixels(...)` at line 329, the code checks `if (this.isTerminal()) return` then falls through to `if (this.finished)` to call encoder.finish(). The `finished` flag is set by onFinish() synchronously. But the check at line 340 `if (this.finished)` happens after the inner while loop drains pixels — if onFinish() was called during a pushPixels await, the loop will reach `if (this.finished)` and enter the finish path. However if onFinish() was called BEFORE the session started (test: 'encode handler treats finish as closed input') the loop correctly skips pixels. The real risk: between `this.state = 'finalising'` (line 343) and the timeout race starting (line 354), a concurrent onCancel() can arrive. onCancel calls finishSession('cancelled') which sets sessionEnded=true. Then when the timeout or finish() resolves, feedEncoder throws (on timeout path) but the `throw err` at line 369 propagates to run()'s catch which calls failSession — but failSession checks isTerminal() and returns early (sessionEnded is already true from onCancel). So the double-terminal is handled. The actual gap: the finish timer is captured as `finishTimer` in the outer scope but `clearTimeout(finishTimer)` is in the `finally` — if onCancel fires and resolveStartPromise/finishSession runs before the timeout fires, the timeout will still fire and call reject, but the promise race is already settled so it's harmless. The `finishTimer` reference captured by the timeout closure is valid because it's in the same closure scope.

title: EncodeHandler constructor fires run() with .catch() but the catch calls failSession which itself calls disposeActiveEncoder with void — if encoder.dispose() throws, the rejection is silently swallowed
description: The constructor calls `this.run().catch((err) => this.failSession(...))`. Inside run()'s finally, `await this.disposeActiveEncoder()` is called. If encoder.dispose() rejects, disposeActiveEncoder catches it with console.error and resolves — so that's safe. But in the timeout/cancel path within feedEncoder (line 368), `void this.disposeActiveEncoder(undefined, true)` is fired and the throw propagates to run()'s catch, which calls failSession. failSession at line 494 also fires `void this.disposeActiveEncoder()`. Since disposePromise is already set (from the void call in feedEncoder's catch), the second call returns the same promise — the rejection from encoder.cancel() is only caught by the first awaiter in disposeActiveEncoder's IIFE. The second void call is fine. However the IIFE structure of disposeActiveEncoder swallows all rejections from both cancel() and dispose() via console.error, meaning transport errors are only logged, not surfaced to the session error path.

title: handleDecodeStart reads hasAnySession synchronously before registering in pendingDecodeStarts, creating a window where a duplicate decode_start for the same sessionId passes the guard
description: handleDecodeStart() checks `hasAnySession(msg.sessionId)` at line 341, then registers `pendingDecodeStarts.set(msg.sessionId, startPromise)` at line 359. The function is async and the check+register span two sequential synchronous statements before any await, so in a single-threaded JS environment there is no interleaving between them. However, handleDecodeStart is called via `void handleDecodeStart(msg)` from the message handler — two rapid decode_start messages with the same sessionId will both hit the message handler synchronously before either async body continues. The first call proceeds past hasAnySession (map is empty), reaches `pendingDecodeStarts.set`, then yields at `getWasm()`. The second call arrives synchronously in the same event loop turn: hasAnySession now returns true (pendingDecodeStarts has the entry), so it correctly sends DuplicateSession. This is safe because the entire synchronous preamble (check + set) completes before the first yield. HOWEVER: the epoch counter `_nextStartEpoch++` at line 365 also happens before the first yield, so the epoch is correctly incremented for both starts if they were somehow reentrant — but they are not because JS is single-threaded. Net: this is actually safe, but the code comment at line 352-356 describing the warm-cache race is the real concern: if getWasm() resolves synchronously (wasmModule !== null), the ENTIRE IIFE body executes synchronously as a microtask chain before the second message is processed. The second decode_start arrives in the next macrotask turn, so hasAnySession will see the handler already in decodeSessions. This is correct. Confidence lowered to low — flagging for verifier review.

title: doShutdown awaits pendingDecodeStarts/pendingEncodeStarts snapshots but new starts registered after the await can be missed, leaking their handlers unfinalised
description: doShutdown() captures `[...pendingDecodeStarts.values(), ...pendingEncodeStarts.values()]` at the moment of the await at line 522-525. After setting shuttingDown=true, new decode_start/encode_start messages are blocked at line 297 (`if (shuttingDown && msg.type !== 'worker_shutdown') return`). So no new starts can arrive after shuttingDown=true. However, any starts that were already in-flight (their IIFE started before shuttingDown was set) will proceed to create handlers after the await resolves. Those handlers are created after the snapshot was taken but before the cancel loop at line 527. The IIFE checks `abortedStarts.delete(msg.sessionId) || shuttingDown` at line 396 — shuttingDown is true so they skip handler creation and just call resolveStartPromise(). This makes the snapshot-then-await pattern safe. BUT: the abortedStarts check uses the current value of shuttingDown (true), so in-flight IIFEs correctly abort. The residual risk: between setting shuttingDown=true (line 519) and the Promise.allSettled await (line 522), if an IIFE had already passed the shuttingDown check at line 396 (read shuttingDown=false), it will create a handler AFTER the snapshot. This handler will not be in the cancel loop. The sequence: (1) IIFE reads shuttingDown=false at line 396, (2) doShutdown sets shuttingDown=true, (3) doShutdown snapshots pending starts (IIFE's promise is included), (4) IIFE creates handler and calls resolveStartPromise(), (5) Promise.allSettled resolves, (6) cancel loop iterates decodeSessions — the handler IS in decodeSessions (set at line 405), so it WILL be cancelled. This is actually safe because decodeSessions is snapped at the cancel loop iteration time (lines 527-532), not snapped before the await. Confidence: low.

title: feedDecoder inner loop checks paused flag after await decoder.push() without re-entering the outer loop's waitForResume path, so a resume-then-pause sequence during a push can leave paused=true with the loop not waiting
description: The inner while loop at line 362 checks `if (this.paused) break` before each chunk, but after `await decoder.push(chunk)` resumes (microtask boundary), the code checks paused again via `if (this.paused) break`. If onPause() fires during decoder.push(), paused is set to true; after push() resumes, the break exits the inner loop. The outer loop then checks `if (this.paused)` at line 350 and enters waitForResume(). This is correct. However, if the sequence is: (1) inner loop checks paused=false, (2) outer loop outer iteration starts, (3) onResume() fires setting paused=false, (4) onPause() fires AGAIN setting paused=true, (5) the inner loop drains, then the outer loop checks paused=true and waits. The wake() call in onPause() (line 229) wakes the outer waitForChunk, so the outer loop will see paused=true and call waitForResume(). This appears correct. The edge: wakeResolve and resumeResolve are separate single-slot promises. If multiple wake() or wakeResume() calls happen before the loop consumes them, only the first resolve is preserved (the others null-check and find resolve already null). This means rapid pause/resume/pause sequences may lose a wake signal. Specifically: wakeResume() nullifies resumeResolve then calls resolve(); a subsequent onResume() that sets paused=false and calls wakeResume() will find resumeResolve=null (no-op) — but the loop is already running (the first wakeResume woke it). The second onResume is delivered while the loop is not waiting, so it's a no-op appropriately. This is actually safe for normal usage but the lost-wake risk applies if pause/resume events arrive faster than the microtask loop processes them.

category: shared mutable state / pre-allocated message objects
title: Pre-allocated _metricMsg/_drainMsg objects are mutated in-place before postMessage — safe per spec comment, but SharedArrayBuffer pixel paths expose the same pattern to a real copy hazard if SAB is ever used for messages
description: The comment at line 149-150 explains the pattern is safe because 'postMessage() performs a synchronous structured clone before returning.' This is correct for regular ArrayBuffers and plain objects. However, if a future maintainer adds a transferable field to _metricMsg or _drainMsg (e.g. metrics containing pixel data), the post-mutation assumption breaks because transferred buffers are detached synchronously and the next mutation would write to a zero-length buffer. The current code is correct. This is flagged as a maintenance hazard: the safety invariant is underdocumented (no JSDoc on the fields explaining WHY mutation-after-post is safe), making it easy to break. Additionally, the SAB handling in toTransferablePixels is correct (copies SAB to regular ArrayBuffer for transfer), but the pre-allocated _drainMsg.queuedBytes field is a number — no buffer involved. Low actual risk; flagged as opportunity for documentation.

title: run()'s Promise.all([feedDecoder, readDecoderEvents]) — if readDecoderEvents throws, feedDecoder is abandoned without cancellation, keeping the decoder's async iterator alive indefinitely
description: run() uses `await Promise.all([this.feedDecoder(decoder), this.readDecoderEvents(decoder)])`. Promise.all rejects as soon as either promise rejects, but the other promise continues running. If readDecoderEvents rejects, feedDecoder continues its loop (calling decoder.push()) until the finally block calls disposeActiveDecoder(). disposeActiveDecoder() sets this.decoder = null and calls decoder.dispose() — but feedDecoder holds a local reference to the same decoder object (passed as parameter), and is still calling decoder.push() on it. If dispose() is async, this creates a race between an in-flight push() and dispose(). The finishSession() call in finally sets this.ended=true and calls wake(), which will eventually break feedDecoder out of its loop after the current await decoder.push() completes. This is an inherent race in the Promise.all pattern with shared resources — dispose and push can overlap. The fix would be to use Promise.allSettled or explicitly signal feedDecoder to stop before disposing.

title: run()'s Promise.all([feedEncoder, readEncoderChunks]) has the same abandoned-task hazard as decode-handler — if readEncoderChunks throws, feedEncoder continues pushing pixels to an encoder being disposed
description: Same structural issue as concurrency-9. If readEncoderChunks rejects (e.g. a chunk iterator throws), Promise.all rejects immediately but feedEncoder continues its loop. The finally block calls `await this.disposeActiveEncoder()`, which calls encoder.cancel() (if cancelFirst) then encoder.dispose(). Meanwhile feedEncoder still holds a reference to the encoder local variable and may be in the middle of `await encoder.pushPixels(...)`. If disposeActiveEncoder completes before feedEncoder's in-flight pushPixels returns, the encoder is disposed while a push is outstanding. The isTerminal() check after pushPixels at line 337 will fire (finishSession was called in the finally, which sets sessionEnded=true), but the dispose has already run.

title: No AbortSignal or cancellation token threads through feedDecoder/readDecoderEvents — cancellation relies on polling this.ended after every await boundary
description: The decode loop checks this.ended/this.cancelled after each await, relying on the single-slot wakeResolve/resumeResolve pattern to unblock sleeping paths. This works correctly today but is brittle: any new await added inside feedDecoder or readDecoderEvents that does NOT check isTerminal() afterward will become a silent hang point. A lightweight AbortSignal or explicit cancellation token passed through the main decode loops would make the cancellation contract explicit and enforceable at every await site. This is an architecture opportunity, not a current bug.

title: handleReleaseState cancels handlers with void decode.onCancel() but does not await, so decodeSessions.delete runs before onCancel's disposeActiveDecoder resolves
description: handleReleaseState() calls `void decode.onCancel('release_state').catch(() => undefined)` then immediately calls `decodeSessions.delete(sessionId)`. onCancel is async and calls disposeActiveDecoder() after finishSession. The delete is fine for Map membership purposes, but the handler continues running asynchronously (its run() loop is still active until disposeActiveDecoder completes). Any further messages for this sessionId that arrive after the delete will be silently dropped (no handler, no pending start). This is the intended behavior — release_state is a hard teardown. However, if a new decode_start arrives before the old handler's run() finally block has cleaned up, the new handler will be created for the same sessionId while the old handler is still executing dispose. The old handler will call callbacks.onSessionEnd (which tries to delete from decodeSessions — the new handler's entry) after the new handler is registered. The onSessionEnd callback is `(sessionId) => decodeSessions.delete(sessionId)` — this would delete the NEW handler's registration. Epoch tokens protect the start IIFE but not the onSessionEnd callback, which has no epoch check.

title: onSessionEnd callback decodeSessions.delete has no epoch guard — a stale handler finishing after release_state+new-start will delete the new handler's map entry
description: The onSessionEnd callback is registered as `(sessionId) => decodeSessions.delete(sessionId)`. If a session is released via handleReleaseState (which deletes the handler and starts a new one), and the old handler's run() loop takes time to finish (e.g. decoder.dispose() is slow), its onSessionEnd fires and deletes whatever is currently in decodeSessions under that sessionId — which may now be the NEW handler. This means the new handler becomes invisible to the router (routeDecodeMessage won't find it), so all subsequent messages for the new session are silently dropped. The fix: the callback should only delete the entry if it still points to the same handler instance, e.g. `if (decodeSessions.get(sessionId) === handler) decodeSessions.delete(sessionId)`.

title: onSessionEnd callback encodeSessions.delete has the same no-epoch-guard race as concurrency-13 for encode sessions
description: Identical to concurrency-13 but for the encode session map. If handleReleaseState cancels an encode session and immediately a new encode_start registers a handler for the same sessionId, the old handler's onSessionEnd fires and deletes the new handler's entry from encodeSessions. All subsequent encode_pixels/encode_finish/encode_cancel messages for the new session are silently dropped. Fix: `if (encodeSessions.get(sessionId) === handler) encodeSessions.delete(sessionId)`.

category: missing test coverage for concurrency scenarios
title: No test covers the release_state + immediate new decode_start race that triggers the onSessionEnd stale-delete bug (concurrency-13/14)
description: The test suite covers cancel-during-pause, budget-exceeded, partial-stride, and release_state suppression, but there is no test that simulates: (1) session in progress, (2) release_state arrives cancelling it, (3) new decode_start for the same sessionId arrives immediately after, (4) verify new handler receives chunks correctly. This sequence is the exact scenario where the onSessionEnd stale-delete can corrupt the new handler's map entry. Adding this test would catch concurrency-13 and concurrency-14 as regressions.
